meta.title: 4.1 What a Coding Agent Actually Is
meta.description: Understand the Observe-Think-Act-Evaluate loop that distinguishes coding agents from code generators — and why tool ownership, multi-step state persistence, and self-correction on failure are what make Make's agent fundamentally different from a chatbot that writes code.

blocks.prompt:
> ChatGPT can write code. Claude Code can write code. Cursor can write code. What makes Claude Code an "agent" and ChatGPT (in a browser) not one?

Take 60 seconds and write your answer before reading.

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module gives you the mental model that separates a coding agent from a code-writing chatbot. By the end, you'll be able to explain — precisely — what the agent loop is, why tool ownership is the defining feature, and what this means for Make's Platform PM role.

A coding agent is not a smarter text generator — it is a model that owns the Observe-Think-Act-Evaluate loop, using tool calls to take real actions in a real environment and recover from real errors. The model is the same; what changes is whether the loop belongs to the human or the machine.

When you paste a function into ChatGPT and ask it to fix a bug, the model generates text that looks like code. You copy it. You paste it. You run it. If it fails, you copy the error back in. Every step of that cycle — reading files, running code, observing results — is performed by you, the human. The model is a sophisticated text completer sitting inside that loop, not driving it.

Claude Code, Cursor, and the agent powering Make are different in kind. They own the loop. They read files, write files, run shell commands, observe the output, make decisions based on what they see, and take the next action — without you touching a keyboard. That cycle is the agent loop, and it is the defining architectural feature of coding agents.

**The agent loop has four phases:**

**Observe.** The agent reads the current state of the world: files on disk, terminal output, compiler errors, test results, the contents of a URL. Crucially, it reads artifacts it created in previous steps. This is what makes step 7 coherent with step 3.

**Think.** Given everything it has observed, the agent reasons about what to do next. This is not a separate "planning" model — it is the same language model, prompted in a way that encourages step-by-step reasoning before action. Chain-of-thought prompting is not a trick; it is how the model simulates deliberation.

**Act.** The agent executes a tool call: write this content to `src/auth/login.tsx`, run `npm test`, query this API endpoint, grep the codebase for all usages of `AuthProvider`. The platform, not the model, executes the action and returns the result.

**Evaluate.** Did it work? The agent reads the tool result — the file contents it just wrote, the test output, the compiler error — and decides whether to proceed, retry, or re-plan. This is the self-correction mechanism that makes agents capable of recovering from their own mistakes.

Then the loop repeats. For a complex feature, this cycle runs 15-40 times.

blocks.question: What is the fundamental difference between a coding assistant (like ChatGPT in a browser) and a coding agent (like Claude Code)? Is it model size, internet access, or something else?
blocks.answer: The fundamental difference is the loop — not the model. A coding assistant generates text that a human must then act on: copy, paste, run, report back. A coding agent owns the full Observe-Think-Act-Evaluate cycle itself, executing tool calls and observing their results without the human performing each step. The same underlying model could power either; what differs is whether the model drives the environment-interaction cycle or sits inside a human-driven one.

blocks.content:
**Tool use is the defining feature.** Without tool use, a model is a text generator. With tool use, it becomes an actor in the world. The agent's "intelligence" is not smarter weights — it is the ability to observe, act, and observe again. This is why researchers frame agency as OODA loops, not as model capability alone.

**State persistence** is the second distinguishing feature. When you are on step 22 of a 30-step implementation, the agent's context window contains what it did at step 8, what error it got at step 11, and what workaround it applied at step 14. It is not starting fresh each turn. It is operating with accumulated situational awareness — which is also why context management (Module 4.3) is one of the hardest platform engineering problems.

## How to Think About This

The agent loop is the same loop you run manually when using a chatbot — read, think, act, check results, repeat. The agent just runs it faster and without requiring you to perform the environment steps. The model capability is nearly identical; the architectural difference is who operates the loop. This is why agent quality depends on tool reliability and context design, not just model intelligence: you can have the world's best reasoner fail if its tools return garbage or its context fills with noise.

**Why this matters for Make:**

Figma Make's agent is not writing code in a vacuum. It is operating inside a live project: a real file system with a real dependency tree, a real build pipeline that either passes or fails, a real preview environment that either renders or breaks. Every action has side effects. Every tool call produces state that constrains the next decision. The agent must manage this complexity coherently across dozens of steps.

A chatbot that generates code-as-text cannot do this. It does not know that the import it suggested in step 4 will cause a circular dependency it encounters at step 19. The agent — running in a real environment, observing real errors — can catch that. This is the capability gap that Make's Platform PM is responsible for.

---

blocks.question:
A user asks Claude Code to "add a dark mode toggle to my app." The agent makes 12 tool calls before finishing. Which of these is NOT a typical agent tool call in that sequence?
A. Read `tailwind.config.js` to understand the current theme configuration
B. Write `src/components/ThemeToggle.tsx` with the new component
C. Run `npm run build` to verify the build passes
D. Call Claude's API to generate a better version of the component it just wrote
blocks.answer:
D.** The agent does not call itself recursively through the API — it *is* the model. Its "self-improvement" comes from reading the results of its own actions and adjusting, not from spawning sub-calls to the inference endpoint. (Multi-agent architectures do spawn sub-calls, but that is a separate pattern from within-session self-correction.)

---

blocks.title:
Open Claude Code in an existing project (or clone any open-source project). Ask it to add a single, small feature — a button, a form field, a new route. Watch the tool use panel. Count how many distinct tool calls it makes before it considers the task done. Note which tool it uses most frequently and which produces the most complex output. Write down what would have happened if any single tool call had failed silently.

---
blocks.solution: You should see a mix of read and write tool calls — typically file reads early (to understand existing structure), followed by file writes (to create or modify components), followed by shell commands (npm run build or similar). A small feature commonly triggers 5–15 tool calls. The most frequent tool is usually file read, and the most complex output is often a shell command that returns compiler output or test results. If any single tool call had failed silently — say, the file write returned success but wrote nothing — the agent would have proceeded assuming a valid state that did not exist. The build step several turns later would have failed with a confusing error pointing at an import of a file that appeared to be written but was actually empty. This is the core reason tool reliability (timeouts, structured errors, truncation signals) matters: silent failures produce confident agents building on broken foundations.

blocks.prompt:
Without looking at the lesson, explain the four phases of the agent loop to someone who uses ChatGPT daily. What would you say is the single thing they would need to add to ChatGPT to make it an agent? Be precise.

---

questions: Think of a time you used an AI coding assistant and had to manually copy-paste code, run it yourself, and copy back errors. How many times did the loop repeat before you got a working result? Which steps in that cycle would you have most wanted the agent to own — and which would you have wanted to keep control of?

Recall a project where something broke at a late step because of a decision made early in the build. Which phase of the agent loop — Observe, Think, Act, or Evaluate — do you think would have caught it earliest? What does that tell you about where current coding agents are most brittle?

---

blocks.content:
A coding agent is not a smarter text generator — it is a model that owns the Observe-Think-Act-Evaluate loop, using tool calls to take real actions in a real environment and recover from real errors.

---

blocks.prompt:
> The agent "acts" by making tool calls. But what is a tool call, exactly? How does the model tell the platform what to do — and how does the platform decide whether to allow it?

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: What are the four phases of the agent loop that distinguish a coding agent from a chatbot?
reviewQuestions.explanation: The agent loop has four phases: Observe (read current state), Think (reason about what to do), Act (execute a tool call), and Evaluate (check the result and decide next steps). This loop repeats 15-40 times for a complex feature.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Nice work! Those four phases are the core architecture that separates an agent from a code-writing assistant.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The four phases are Observe, Think, Act, and Evaluate. A chatbot generates text a human must then run and report back — the agent owns this entire cycle itself, looping through it 15-40 times for a complex task.

reviewQuestions.question: Which of the following correctly describes why tool use is the defining feature of a coding agent?
options: Tool use allows the agent to use a larger context window than a standard chatbot
Without tool use, a model is a text generator — with tool use, it becomes an actor that can observe, act, and observe again in a real environment
Tool use lets the agent access the internet in real time to look up documentation
reviewQuestions.explanation: The lesson states: 'Without tool use, a model is a text generator. With tool use, it becomes an actor in the world.' The agent's intelligence comes from the ability to execute actions and observe their real results — not from a larger model or internet access.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: You've got it! The transition from text generator to actor is exactly what tool use enables.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Context window size and internet access aren't the defining factor. The key is that tool use turns a text generator into an actor — the agent can write a file, run a build, observe the error, and adjust, all without a human performing those steps.

reviewQuestions.question: In the agent loop, approximately how many tool calls does a coding agent typically make for a complex feature implementation?
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module states the agent loop runs '15-40 times' for a complex feature. Each cycle covers one observe-think-act-evaluate pass, and complex tasks like adding authentication require many such cycles.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly right — complex features require many cycles, which is why state persistence across those steps is so critical.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The module specifically says the loop runs 15-40 times for a complex feature. Each cycle is one observe-think-act-evaluate pass, and tasks like adding full authentication across many files require many such cycles.
